title: Panic Disorder: Clark's Cognitive Model
content: Clark's (1986) cognitive model of panic proposes that panic attacks result from the catastrophic misinterpretation of benign bodily sensations. The model has three components: (1) a trigger stimulus (internal — palpitations, dizziness; or external — a shopping mall); (2) perception of this as a threat ('Something is wrong'); (3) apprehension, which amplifies the physical sensation through the autonomic nervous system, creating a positive feedback loop. Key maintaining factors include selective attention to bodily sensations, safety behaviours (sitting down, holding walls), and avoidance of triggering situations. CBT targets the catastrophic misinterpretation directly through verbal restructuring and interoceptive exposure.
short_summary: Explains Clark's 1986 cognitive model of panic: catastrophic misinterpretation of bodily sensations in a self-amplifying feedback loop.
safety_notes: Rule out medical causes for physical sensations before applying the cognitive model.

title: Panic Disorder: Interoceptive Exposure
content: Interoceptive exposure (IE) involves deliberately inducing feared physical sensations to promote habituation and correct catastrophic beliefs. Common IE exercises include: spinning in a chair (dizziness), breathing through a coffee stirrer (shortness of breath), running in place (heart rate increase), and staring at a fixed point (depersonalisation). Each exercise is matched to the client's feared sensations identified during assessment. IE works best when the client enters the exercise predicting a catastrophic outcome ('I will faint/have a heart attack') and remains in the experience long enough for anxiety to naturally decrease. Repeated practice generalises to real-life situations and demonstrates that feared sensations are tolerable and time-limited.
short_summary: Describes interoceptive exposure exercises matched to feared panic sensations, promoting habituation and correcting catastrophic beliefs.
safety_notes: Medical clearance required before inducing physical sensations in clients with cardiac, respiratory, or vestibular conditions.
contraindications: Active cardiac conditions; uncontrolled hypertension; severe respiratory disorders.

title: Panic Disorder: Dropping Safety Behaviours
content: Safety behaviours in panic disorder are responses used to prevent feared outcomes during a panic attack: holding onto surfaces, sitting down, carrying medication, tensing muscles, or leaving the situation. While providing short-term relief, safety behaviours prevent learning that the feared catastrophe would not have occurred without them, thereby maintaining the disorder. CBT intervention involves collaboratively identifying safety behaviours via interview and diary, psychoeducation about their maintaining function, and systematic drop experiments — comparing the experience of panicking with and without safety behaviours. Clients typically report that dropping safety behaviours produces less, not more, distress over time, directly disconfirming the predicted catastrophe.
short_summary: Explains how safety behaviours maintain panic disorder and outlines the drop-experiment approach to systematically eliminating them.

title: Panic Disorder: Situational Avoidance and Agoraphobia
content: Agoraphobia commonly co-occurs with panic disorder and involves avoidance of situations from which escape would be difficult or embarrassing, or help unavailable during a panic attack. Common avoided situations include public transport, supermarkets, queues, open spaces, and driving. CBT addresses agoraphobia via a graduated exposure hierarchy constructed collaboratively with the client. Situations are ranked by anticipated distress (SUDS 0–100), and in-vivo exposure begins at a manageable level. Therapist accompaniment is faded progressively to promote independence. Normalisation of the relationship between panic, avoidance, and increasing life restriction is central to motivating engagement with graded exposure.
short_summary: Describes agoraphobia as a consequence of panic-driven avoidance and outlines graduated in-vivo exposure hierarchy construction.

title: Panic: AWARE Technique for Acute Episodes
content: The AWARE technique provides a structured in-the-moment coping response during panic attacks: Accept the anxiety — acknowledge rather than fight it ('I accept this feeling'); Watch the anxiety — observe it as a detached observer, rating intensity on a 0–10 scale; Act normal — continue whatever activity was underway with slow, paced breathing; Repeat the steps — cycle through acceptance and watching as anxiety rises and falls; Expect the best — remind oneself that panic is time-limited and always passes. AWARE teaches that fighting panic amplifies it, while acceptance and observation activate natural habituation. It is paired with psychoeducation and interoceptive exposure for best effect.
short_summary: Introduces the AWARE five-step coping technique for managing panic attacks through acceptance, observation, and behavioural normalisation.

title: Catastrophising: Best/Worst/Most Likely Technique
content: The best/worst/most likely technique is a structured Socratic method for decatastrophising anxious predictions. The therapist guides the client to: (1) identify the feared outcome ('I will fail the presentation and be humiliated'); (2) identify the best realistic outcome ('The presentation goes well and I receive positive feedback'); (3) identify the most realistic likely outcome, drawing on base rates and evidence ('I will be somewhat nervous but manage reasonably well'); (4) rate the probability of each outcome; (5) develop a coping plan for managing if the worst did occur. Repeated application trains the client to shift spontaneously from catastrophic to realistic appraisal and builds confidence that they could cope with difficult outcomes.
short_summary: Describes the best/worst/most likely technique for decatastrophising anxious predictions by identifying realistic probabilistic outcomes.

title: Catastrophising: Coping Self-Statements
content: Coping self-statements are brief, personalised cognitive cues used to interrupt catastrophic thinking and activate a coping mindset. They differ from positive affirmations in that they acknowledge difficulty while asserting coping ability. Effective coping statements address both the overestimation of threat ('The probability of this happening is very low') and the underestimation of coping ability ('Even if this happened, I could manage — I have coped with difficult things before'). Clients generate personalised statements during therapy, record them on cards or a phone note, and rehearse using them in imagery-based practice. Coping statements are rehearsed in session before being deployed in real-life high-anxiety situations.
short_summary: Explains how personalised coping self-statements counter catastrophising by addressing both threat overestimation and coping capacity underestimation.

title: Catastrophising: Probability Pie Charts
content: The probability pie chart is a visual cognitive restructuring tool for overestimation of threat. The client first states their catastrophic prediction and rates its probability (e.g., 'I will have a heart attack, 80%'). The therapist then guides the client to list all other possible explanations for their symptoms or the anticipated event, rating each independently. The percentages are drawn as slices in a pie chart, with the catastrophic prediction allocated its slice last. This visual representation graphically demonstrates how much probability space is occupied by more likely explanations, reducing the subjective sense that the catastrophe is imminent and highly probable.
short_summary: Introduces probability pie charts as a visual tool for demonstrating overestimation of catastrophic outcomes relative to more likely alternatives.

title: Catastrophising: Decoupling Prediction from Coping Capacity
content: A key maintaining factor in catastrophising is the fused belief that an anticipated negative event would be both highly likely and impossible to cope with. Decoupling separates two independent questions: (1) 'How likely is the feared outcome?' and (2) 'How well could I cope if it did occur?' Many clients overestimate threat AND underestimate coping capacity simultaneously. Therapeutic work addresses coping estimation by reviewing past difficult events successfully managed, identifying existing strengths and resources, and constructing specific coping plans for the feared scenario. When coping confidence increases, even moderate-probability negative events lose their catastrophic quality, reducing anticipatory anxiety significantly.
short_summary: Explains the decoupling technique: separating probability estimation from coping capacity to reduce the catastrophic quality of feared outcomes.

title: Behavioural Activation: Activity Monitoring Forms
content: Activity monitoring is the first step of behavioural activation and involves systematically recording activities hour by hour across a week, along with mood ratings (0–10) for each activity. Analysis of the monitoring form reveals: (1) the actual activity level (often lower than the client estimates); (2) activities associated with better mood (mastery and pleasure activities); (3) patterns of low activity and avoidance; (4) time patterns (e.g., mornings associated with particularly low mood). Monitoring precedes scheduling and gives both client and therapist objective data to guide which activities to target. The monitoring process itself can have a mild activating effect by increasing self-observation and awareness of mood-behaviour links.
short_summary: Describes the activity monitoring form as the data-gathering phase of behavioural activation, linking activities to mood patterns before scheduling.
contraindications: Severe psychomotor retardation — simplify monitoring to 2–3 daily ratings initially.

title: Behavioural Activation: Values-Based Activity Scheduling
content: Values-based behavioural activation moves beyond simply increasing pleasant activities to scheduling activities congruent with the client's core values. Clients complete a values survey identifying what matters most to them (relationships, health, creativity, work, community). Activities are then identified that express each value, rated by importance versus current engagement, and scheduled. Activities connected to values provide a sense of meaning and purpose rather than just temporary pleasure, making them more robust against the low motivation characteristic of depression. Values clarification also addresses nihilism ('Nothing matters anyway') that can undermine standard pleasant activities scheduling by grounding action in what the client genuinely cares about.
short_summary: Extends standard BA to values-based scheduling, targeting meaning and purpose to overcome nihilism and low motivation in depression.

title: Behavioural Activation: Graded Task Assignment
content: Graded task assignment (GTA) addresses the problem of tasks that feel overwhelming by breaking them into a sequence of small, manageable steps. Each step is assigned a predicted difficulty rating, completed, then re-rated for actual difficulty and mood change. Common targets include tasks the client has been avoiding due to depression: returning a phone call, opening post, cleaning a room. GTA works by providing mastery experiences that counter the 'I can't' cognition through direct evidence. The stepwise structure reduces avoidance by making initial steps minimally aversive. Success is reinforced through therapist acknowledgement and the client's own data showing predicted difficulty consistently exceeded actual difficulty.
short_summary: Describes graded task assignment for depression: breaking overwhelming tasks into small steps with predicted vs actual difficulty ratings to build mastery.
source_name: Beck, Rush, Shaw & Emery CBT for Depression

title: Behavioural Activation: Mastery and Pleasure Ratings
content: In behavioural activation, activities are rated on two dimensions: mastery (the sense of accomplishment, even if performance was objectively modest) and pleasure (enjoyment or positive affect). Both are rated 0–10 after each activity. Tracking mastery and pleasure separately is clinically important because: (1) depressed clients often experience mastery before pleasure; (2) mastery activities (e.g., completing a task) can elevate mood even when pleasure is absent; (3) the pattern reveals which activity type is more mood-responsive for a given client. Therapists use mastery and pleasure data to refine activity schedules, increase frequency of high-rated activities, and provide evidence against 'Nothing I do makes me feel better.'
short_summary: Explains mastery and pleasure ratings in BA as two separate mood-relevant dimensions guiding personalised activity selection in depression treatment.
source_name: Beck, Rush, Shaw & Emery CBT for Depression

title: Avoidance: The Anxiety-Avoidance Maintenance Cycle
content: The anxiety-avoidance maintenance cycle is a foundational CBT model: a situation triggers anxiety; avoidance provides immediate relief (negative reinforcement); but avoidance prevents habituation, maintains the belief that the situation is dangerous, and restricts the client's life over time. Each avoided situation reinforces the idea that anxiety is intolerable and the situation is unmanageable. The model is used psychoeducationally to explain why avoidance is self-defeating despite its immediate effectiveness. The therapeutic implication is approach: gradually confronting avoided situations to obtain disconfirming evidence and allow natural habituation. Therapists present this model early in treatment to motivate engagement with exposure work.
short_summary: Explains the anxiety-avoidance maintenance cycle: negative reinforcement of avoidance prevents habituation and progressively expands life restriction.

title: Avoidance: Situational vs Cognitive Avoidance
content: Avoidance takes two primary forms that must both be targeted in CBT. Situational avoidance involves staying away from external triggers: places, people, activities, or objects associated with anxiety or distress. Cognitive avoidance involves mental strategies for reducing contact with distressing thoughts and images: thought suppression, distraction, reassurance-seeking, and worry as a cognitive substitute for imagery. Both forms maintain anxiety by preventing the processing of fear-relevant information. Effective treatment identifies and targets both dimensions. In trauma and PTSD, cognitive avoidance of traumatic memories is particularly important and requires specific imaginal exposure approaches alongside situational exposure.
short_summary: Distinguishes situational avoidance (external triggers) from cognitive avoidance (thought suppression, reassurance-seeking) and their separate treatment implications.

title: Avoidance: Identifying Subtle Avoidance Behaviours
content: Subtle avoidance behaviours are partial-approach actions that reduce engagement with feared situations without complete escape. Examples include: reading during social events rather than participating, wearing earphones in crowded places, making tasks shorter than agreed, mentally rehearsing what to say during conversations, or seeking brief reassurance before completing an exposure. These behaviours reduce anxiety momentarily but prevent the full habituation and belief disconfirmation that complete approach would provide. Assessment of subtle avoidance requires collaborative exploration using in-session observation and functional analysis of what the client actually does within a situation. Once identified, subtle avoidances are faded in a graduated manner alongside standard exposure.
short_summary: Identifies subtle avoidance behaviours (partial approach strategies) that maintain anxiety while appearing like engagement, and describes a collaborative fading approach.

title: Avoidance: Motivational Enhancement for Approach Behaviour
content: Avoidance is strongly negatively reinforced, making motivation to approach the primary early treatment challenge. Motivational enhancement for approach draws on MI techniques: eliciting the client's own change talk about the costs of avoidance; exploring values and goals blocked by avoidance ('What would you do if anxiety weren't in the way?'); using decisional balance to identify short-term gains and long-term costs of avoidance; and reviewing past approach behaviours the client found rewarding. Therapists highlight the discrepancy between the client's values and current avoidance behaviour without confrontation. Building a compelling personal rationale precedes and underpins collaborative construction of an exposure hierarchy.
short_summary: Describes motivational enhancement techniques for building willingness to approach avoided situations using MI and values-cost discrepancy methods.

title: Worry: Type 1 and Type 2 Meta-Worry in GAD
content: Wells's metacognitive model of GAD distinguishes two types of worry. Type 1 worry ('What if my child gets ill?') is ordinary, object-level worry about external events. Type 2 worry — meta-worry — is worry about worry itself ('What if I cannot stop worrying?', 'Worrying is damaging my brain'). Meta-worry triggers catastrophic appraisal of the worry process and motivates unhelpful control strategies (suppression, reassurance-seeking) that paradoxically increase intrusive thoughts. CBT for GAD must address both levels: Type 1 worry via problem-solving and postponement; Type 2 meta-worry via psychoeducation, Socratic questioning of meta-worry beliefs, and loss-of-control experiments demonstrating that worry is controllable.
short_summary: Explains Wells's metacognitive model distinguishing Type 1 (object-level) and Type 2 (meta-worry) in GAD, with treatment approaches for each level.

title: Worry: Productive vs Unproductive Worry Classification
content: CBT for GAD distinguishes productive worry from unproductive worry to help clients relate to their worry more flexibly. Productive worry has a current, solvable problem as its object and leads to problem-solving action. Unproductive worry concerns hypothetical ('what if') scenarios with no immediate practical solution, generating distress without useful outcome. Clients are taught to apply the 'solvability test': 'Is this a current, real problem I can do something about right now?' If yes, it warrants structured problem-solving. If no (hypothetical, future-focused), it warrants postponement, defusion, or mindful letting go. This classification reduces total worry time by directing cognitive energy appropriately rather than applying blanket suppression.
short_summary: Teaches the productive vs unproductive worry distinction so clients direct problem-solving at solvable concerns and use acceptance for hypothetical scenarios.

title: Worry: Intolerance of Uncertainty as a GAD Core Process
content: Intolerance of uncertainty (IU) is a cognitive-dispositional characteristic strongly linked to GAD. Individuals high in IU react negatively to uncertain situations with worry, avoidance, and reassurance-seeking, regardless of actual outcome probability. CBT for IU involves: (1) identifying IU as a transdiagnostic maintaining factor; (2) examining the beliefs about uncertainty ('If I worry enough, I will be prepared for anything'); (3) behavioural experiments deliberately introducing uncertainty (making decisions without rechecking, tolerating ambiguous responses, refraining from reassurance-seeking); (4) challenging the belief that uncertainty is dangerous rather than merely uncomfortable. Targeted IU treatment produces significant GAD symptom reduction.
short_summary: Addresses intolerance of uncertainty as a core GAD maintaining process with targeted behavioural experiments for tolerating unresolved situations.

title: Worry: Imagery Rescripting for Worst-Case Worry Scenarios
content: Many chronic worriers maintain vivid mental images of worst-case scenarios that generate persistent anxiety. Imagery rescripting for worry involves: (1) identifying the core worry image in detail; (2) rating distress associated with the image; (3) guiding the client through a rescripted version in which coping, support, or a different outcome replaces the catastrophic ending; (4) anchoring the rescripted image through repetition. The technique reduces the salience and distress of worry images without suppressing them. It is particularly effective where worry contains a core feared image (e.g., a health catastrophe, a relationship ending, a humiliating failure). Rescripting is combined with cognitive restructuring for best effect.
short_summary: Describes imagery rescripting for chronic worry: replacing catastrophic worst-case mental images with coping-focused alternatives to reduce their distress.
source_name: Hackmann, Bennett-Levy & Holmes Oxford Guide to Imagery
safety_notes: Imagery work near trauma material should proceed with trauma-informed caution.
contraindications: Active PTSD or trauma symptoms — use imagery work under specialist supervision.

title: Rumination: Understanding the Ruminative Response Style
content: Nolen-Hoeksema's response styles theory identifies rumination — repetitive, passive focus on symptoms of distress and their causes, meanings, and consequences — as a key transdiagnostic maintenance factor, particularly in depression. The ruminative response style is contrasted with distraction (turning attention outward) and problem-solving (active engagement). Rumination extends the duration of depressive episodes, impairs problem-solving, increases negative thinking, and reduces social support. Assessment uses the Ruminative Response Scale (RRS). CBT targets rumination through metacognitive awareness, attentional retraining, behavioural activation to interrupt ruminative cycles, and mindfulness-based techniques that build flexible attention.
short_summary: Explains Nolen-Hoeksema's ruminative response theory as a depression maintenance factor and outlines CBT assessment and treatment targets.

title: Rumination: Concreteness Training
content: Concreteness training (CNT) is an evidence-based intervention for rumination developed by Watkins. It involves training the client to shift from abstract, evaluative processing ('Why am I such a failure?') to concrete, specific, how-based processing ('What exactly happened, step by step?'). Abstract thinking maintains rumination because it generates sweeping negative conclusions rather than specific actionable information. Concreteness exercises include: imagining recent events in vivid sensory detail rather than evaluatively; practising step-by-step problem descriptions; and using 'How?' rather than 'Why?' questions when analysing difficulties. CNT has demonstrated efficacy in reducing depressive rumination and improving problem-solving in both individual and self-help formats.
short_summary: Describes Watkins's concreteness training (CNT) for depression: shifting from abstract 'why' rumination to specific 'how' processing to break depressive cycles.

title: Rumination: Attentional Training Technique
content: Wells's Attention Training Technique (ATT) is a metacognitive therapy procedure designed to strengthen voluntary control of attention and reduce self-focused rumination. In ATT, the client is guided through an auditory attention exercise: selective attention (focusing on one of multiple environmental sounds); rapid switching between sounds; and divided attention (broadening to attend to multiple sounds simultaneously). ATT is practised daily for 10–15 minutes. The rationale is that rumination involves a locked, self-focused attention mode; ATT trains flexible redeployment of attention away from internal self-evaluation. ATT is an adjunct to cognitive restructuring and does not replace content-level cognitive work.
short_summary: Explains Wells's Attention Training Technique (ATT) — an auditory attention exercise — as a metacognitive therapy tool for reducing self-focused rumination.

title: Rumination: Behavioural Antidotes to Ruminative Cycles
content: Rumination commonly escalates during low-activity periods, especially unstructured time at home. Behavioural antidotes involve scheduling absorbing activities that occupy attentional capacity and interrupt the ruminative loop. Effective antidote activities have the following properties: require active cognitive engagement (not passive media consumption); are externally rather than internally focused; provide sensory or social stimulation; and are connected to personal interest or meaning. Examples include practical tasks, social contact, physical exercise, craft activities, and playing music. Clients are taught to notice rumination onset cues and immediately implement a pre-selected antidote activity, rather than attempting to think their way out of the ruminative cycle.
short_summary: Describes scheduling absorbing, externally-focused activities as practical behavioural antidotes to rumination triggered during low-activity periods.

title: Sleep Anxiety: Pre-Sleep Cognitive Arousal
content: Pre-sleep cognitive arousal involves elevated mental activity in the period before sleep onset, characterised by intrusive thoughts, planning, worry, and self-monitoring. Harvey's cognitive model of insomnia identifies pre-sleep worry as a primary trigger for physiological arousal, which in turn delays sleep onset. Common pre-sleep worry themes include: worry about sleep itself ('I won't sleep and I'll function badly tomorrow'), daytime performance worries (work, relationships), and physiological monitoring (checking heart rate, body temperature). CBT-I addresses pre-sleep cognitive arousal through worry postponement, constructive worry scheduling earlier in the evening, and stimulus control instructions that reduce cognitive activation in the bedroom.
short_summary: Explains Harvey's cognitive model: pre-sleep worry triggers physiological arousal that delays sleep onset, addressed by worry postponement and stimulus control.

title: Sleep Anxiety: Stimulus Control Therapy
content: Stimulus control therapy (SCT) is one of the most effective single-component treatments for insomnia. It works by re-associating the bed and bedroom with sleepiness rather than wakefulness, worry, or frustration. Key instructions: (1) go to bed only when sleepy (not merely tired); (2) use the bed only for sleep and sex; (3) if unable to sleep after approximately 20 minutes, leave the bed and engage in a low-arousal activity, returning only when sleepy again; (4) get up at the same time every morning regardless of sleep duration; (5) avoid daytime napping. SCT breaks the conditioned arousal response to the bedroom environment that maintains chronic insomnia and is combined with sleep restriction and cognitive restructuring in full CBT-I.
short_summary: Describes stimulus control therapy for insomnia: re-associating the bedroom with sleepiness by restricting bed use to sleep only and maintaining consistent wake times.
contraindications: Elderly clients with fall risk — modify the 'leave the bed' instruction for safety.

title: Sleep Anxiety: Challenging Dysfunctional Sleep Beliefs
content: Dysfunctional beliefs about sleep maintain insomnia by generating pre-sleep anxiety and catastrophic responses to wakefulness. Common beliefs targeted in CBT-I include: 'I need exactly 8 hours to function' (sleep need is variable and individual); 'If I don't sleep I will have a breakdown' (consequences of one night's poor sleep are typically modest); 'I can never sleep without medication' (attribution that undermines self-efficacy); 'The night is ruined if I wake up' (nocturnal awakenings are physiologically normal). The Dysfunctional Beliefs and Attitudes about Sleep scale (DBAS) assesses these beliefs. Restructuring involves psychoeducation, conducting sleep experiments across multiple nights, and reviewing functional evidence.
short_summary: Identifies dysfunctional sleep beliefs assessed by DBAS and CBT-I restructuring strategies using psychoeducation and sleep experiment evidence.

title: Sleep Anxiety: Constructive Worry and Pre-Bed Wind-Down
content: Constructive worry scheduling involves deliberately engaging with worries and to-do items earlier in the evening (typically 1–2 hours before bed) to prevent them from intruding during the pre-sleep period. The client writes down: outstanding worries; tasks for tomorrow; and any pending decisions. By externalising these items in a designated notebook, the mind receives a signal that they are captured and do not require active processing during the night. This is combined with a wind-down routine: 30–60 minutes of low-arousal activities (light reading, stretching, a warm bath) signalling the transition from wakefulness to sleep. Both strategies are protective behavioural components of CBT-I that directly reduce night-time cognitive arousal.
short_summary: Describes constructive worry scheduling and pre-bed wind-down routine as CBT-I protective behaviours that reduce cognitive arousal before sleep.

title: Self-Criticism: The Self-Critical Chain Analysis
content: The self-critical chain analysis maps the sequence from triggering event to self-critical conclusion. Steps: (1) identify the activating event (e.g., making a mistake at work); (2) identify the automatic self-critical thought ('I am incompetent'); (3) identify the emotional consequence (shame, anxiety); (4) identify the behavioural response (withdrawal, overworking to compensate); (5) identify the core belief reinforced ('I am fundamentally flawed'). The chain illustrates how a discrete event becomes linked to a global negative self-evaluation generating disproportionate distress. Identifying the chain allows CBT intervention at multiple points: at the automatic thought level, at the conditional rule, and at the core belief level for more durable change.
short_summary: Introduces self-critical chain analysis mapping trigger → self-critical thought → emotion → behaviour → core belief reinforcement as intervention targets.

title: Self-Criticism: Compassionate Mind Training
content: Compassionate Mind Training (CMT), developed by Paul Gilbert, targets the high shame and self-criticism found in clients whose early caregiving was absent, critical, or abusive. CMT involves: psychoeducation on the evolved nature of self-criticism (threat system dominance from early experience); imagery exercises developing a compassionate ideal figure; compassionate letter writing (addressing oneself as a compassionate friend would); chair work contrasting the self-critical and compassionate voices; and soothing physiology techniques (slow breathing, warm posture, gentle tone of voice). The goal is to activate the soothing/affiliation system, reducing threat-based self-evaluation and generating feelings of warmth, safety, and self-acceptance.
short_summary: Explains Gilbert's Compassionate Mind Training: activating the soothing system through imagery, letter writing, and chair work to counter chronic self-criticism.
safety_notes: Imagery work may be distressing for clients with complex trauma — proceed gradually with clinical supervision.
contraindications: Active psychosis; severe dissociation without specialist support.

title: Self-Criticism: Recognising Critical Thinking Patterns
content: Self-critical thinking involves characteristic cognitive distortion patterns extending beyond ordinary negative evaluation. Common patterns include: over-generalisation ('I always fail'); labelling ('I am a loser' rather than 'I made a mistake'); mind-reading ('Everyone thinks I am incompetent'); discounting positives ('That was just luck'); comparative thinking ('Others do this effortlessly'); and applying double standards (stricter criteria to oneself than to others in identical situations). CBT identifies these patterns using thought records and the cognitive distortions framework. Clients learn to name the distortion type when a self-critical thought arises, creating metacognitive distance that opens space for a balanced alternative perspective.
short_summary: Identifies self-critical thinking patterns (over-generalisation, labelling, double standards) and CBT methods for recognising and naming them to create cognitive distance.

title: Self-Criticism: The Double Standard Technique
content: The double standard technique is one of the most effective CBT interventions for self-criticism. It targets the common phenomenon where clients apply harsher standards to themselves than to others in identical situations. The therapist asks: 'If your close friend made the same mistake, what would you say to them?' Clients typically respond with much greater kindness and perspective when addressing others. This vivid contrast makes the double standard experientially visible. Follow-up questions explore what prevents applying the same compassion to oneself (core beliefs, fear of self-indulgence). The client is guided to write a response to their own self-criticism using the voice they would use with a close friend, integrating this practice into regular thought record work.
short_summary: Describes the double standard technique: surfacing the harsher standards applied to oneself versus others and developing a compassionate friend-voice alternative.
source_name: Burns Feeling Good; Beck Institute CBT Principles

title: Perfectionism: The Procrastination-Perfectionism Link
content: Perfectionism is a primary driver of procrastination. The mechanism involves: high standards creating anticipated fear of failure; this fear triggering avoidance of starting tasks; avoidance reducing anxiety short-term while creating deadline pressure long-term. The result is a cycle of procrastination, late or incomplete work, confirmation of failure fears, and deepened perfectionism. CBT addresses perfectionism-driven procrastination via: psychoeducation on the perfectionism-avoidance link; behavioural experiments producing 'good enough' work and observing actual outcomes; graded task initiation (starting with 5 minutes rather than waiting for perfect conditions); and cognitive restructuring of all-or-nothing task initiation beliefs.
short_summary: Explains the perfectionism-driven procrastination cycle and CBT interventions including good-enough experiments and graded task initiation strategies.
source_name: Shafran, Egan & Wade CBT for Perfectionism

title: Perfectionism: All-or-Nothing Thinking and the Continuum Technique
content: All-or-nothing (dichotomous) thinking is the cognitive foundation of maladaptive perfectionism: evaluating performance as either 'perfect/success' or 'imperfect/failure' with no middle ground. CBT for perfectionism targets this thinking style through: the continuum technique (placing performance on a 0–100% scale rather than binary evaluation); generating multiple gradations of outcome; reviewing how the client would rate identical performance in others; and examining the costs of binary evaluation. Clients keep a 'shades of grey' diary recording imperfect actions they completed and rating actual versus feared consequences. The exercise typically reveals that real consequences of imperfection are far less severe than predicted.
short_summary: Addresses all-or-nothing thinking in perfectionism using the continuum technique and a shades-of-grey diary for developing nuanced performance evaluation.
source_name: Shafran, Egan & Wade CBT for Perfectionism

title: Perfectionism: Behavioural Experiments for Lowering Standards
content: Behavioural experiments for perfectionism test the prediction that lowering standards will lead to catastrophic consequences. Common experiments include: submitting a report at 80% effort and observing feedback; replying to emails without repeated editing; completing a task at 'good enough' and monitoring actual self-satisfaction and external response. Experiments are designed collaboratively with predicted and actual outcomes recorded. Results typically disconfirm the perfectionistic prediction that imperfection will be noticed catastrophically. Experiments also test the secondary belief that high standards are solely responsible for past successes, generating consideration of alternative explanations: effort, timing, relationships, and circumstance.
short_summary: Describes perfectionism-targeted behavioural experiments testing the prediction that 'good enough' performance produces catastrophic outcomes.
source_name: Shafran, Egan & Wade CBT for Perfectionism

title: Perfectionism: Broadening the Self-Worth Base
content: A central feature of clinical perfectionism is the near-exclusive basing of self-worth on achievement and performance. This means the entire self-concept is hostage to task outcomes, creating chronic vulnerability to shame on any failure. CBT for perfectionism addresses the self-worth domain by: mapping the client's self-evaluation domains (what percentages of self-worth come from work, relationships, health, character, community, etc.); identifying the over-reliance on achievement; and broadening the self-evaluation base through investment in currently neglected domains. This reduces the emotional stakes of individual failures and distributes self-worth across more stable foundations. The 'pie chart of self-worth' is used as a visual tool for this exercise.
short_summary: Addresses achievement-based self-worth in perfectionism using a self-evaluation domains pie chart to distribute self-worth across more stable foundations.
source_name: Shafran, Egan & Wade CBT for Perfectionism

title: Social Anxiety: Post-Event Processing
content: Post-event processing (PEP) is a form of negative ruminative review that occurs after social interactions, in which individuals with social anxiety replay the event focusing on perceived failures, embarrassing moments, and evidence of negative evaluation. Clark and Wells identified PEP as a key maintaining factor because it: selectively attends to negative aspects; constructs a distorted performance memory feeding the feared self-image of the next social situation; and primes heightened self-focused attention in future events. CBT for social anxiety addresses PEP through: psychoeducation; postponement contracts (banning PEP for 48 hours after events); video feedback providing objective performance evidence; and compassionate reviewing of social events.
short_summary: Explains post-event processing as a social anxiety maintenance factor and CBT strategies including PEP postponement and video feedback.

title: Social Anxiety: Video Feedback for Self-Image Updating
content: Clients with social anxiety hold a distorted negative self-image of how they appear to others, constructed from internal bodily sensations and past failures rather than actual observed performance. Video feedback is a behavioural experiment providing objective external evidence. Procedure: (1) client identifies their feared self-image ('I come across as visibly shaking and incoherent'); (2) client predicts how the video will look before viewing; (3) video of a social performance is reviewed; (4) client rates how they actually appeared; (5) the discrepancy between feared prediction and actual performance is processed cognitively. Video feedback consistently reduces the feared self-image rating and increases social self-confidence through direct disconfirmatory evidence.
short_summary: Describes the video feedback protocol for social anxiety: comparing feared self-image predictions against actual video-recorded performance to update distorted self-evaluation.

title: Social Anxiety: Shifting Attention Outward
content: Self-focused attention is a central maintaining factor in social anxiety: the client directs attention inward (monitoring internal sensations, rehearsing speech, assessing performance) rather than outward (engaging with others, the conversation, the environment). This self-monitoring amplifies awareness of anxiety symptoms, reduces performance quality, and prevents processing of disconfirming information about social situations. CBT treatment involves attention-shifting training: learning to deliberately focus externally — on the person being spoken to, on what they are actually saying, on the surrounding environment. Clients practise this during low-anxiety social situations first, then in progressively more challenging contexts, paired with safety behaviour elimination.
short_summary: Targets self-focused attention in social anxiety via externally-directed attention training during graded social exposures combined with safety behaviour fading.

title: Social Anxiety: Feared Self-Image and Compassionate Reappraisal
content: The feared self-image in social anxiety is a vivid, distressing internal representation of how the client believes they appear to others — typically imagined as visibly anxious, incompetent, or ridiculous. This image is maintained by safety behaviours, avoidance of disconfirmatory information, and post-event processing. CBT addresses the feared self-image through: imagery work exploring its origins (often traceable to a specific humiliating social experience); compassionate reappraisal of the past scene from an adult perspective; testing the current feared image against video feedback; and developing a realistic, compassionate observer image to replace it. Shifting from the feared to a realistic self-image produces significant social anxiety reduction.
short_summary: Addresses the feared self-image in social anxiety through imagery origin work, compassionate adult reappraisal, and video feedback disconfirmation.
source_name: Clark & Wells Social Anxiety Model; Hackmann et al.

title: Metacognitive Therapy: Detached Mindfulness
content: Detached mindfulness (DM) is a metacognitive therapy technique developed by Wells for interrupting the cognitive attentional syndrome (CAS) — the combination of worry, rumination, threat monitoring, and unhelpful coping behaviours that maintain psychological disorders. DM involves adopting a metacognitive stance: aware of thoughts as mental events without engaging with, evaluating, or attempting to control them. The client observes thoughts as background noise — like passing cars heard from an upstairs window — without directing attention toward them for analysis. DM differs from standard mindfulness in that it specifically targets the over-engaged response to negative thoughts rather than cultivating general present-moment awareness. It is particularly effective for persistent worry and rumination.
short_summary: Explains Wells's detached mindfulness technique: observing thoughts as passing mental events without engagement to interrupt the worry-rumination attentional syndrome.
contraindications: Active psychosis — use with clinical supervision.

title: Grounding Techniques for Acute Anxiety
content: Grounding techniques orient the individual to present-moment sensory experience, interrupting anxious cognitive loops and hyperarousal states. Common evidence-informed methods include: the 5-4-3-2-1 technique (identify 5 things seen, 4 heard, 3 touched, 2 smelled, 1 tasted); physical grounding (pressing feet firmly into the floor, holding a cold object); orienting questions ('What day is it? Where am I? What is around me?'); and box breathing (4 counts inhale, 4 hold, 4 exhale, 4 hold). Grounding works by redirecting attentional resources from internal threat-focused processing to current external sensory input, activating the parasympathetic system and reducing physiological arousal. Grounding is a first-line coping skill, not a standalone treatment for anxiety disorders.
short_summary: Describes evidence-informed grounding techniques (5-4-3-2-1, physical grounding, box breathing) for interrupting acute anxiety and hyperarousal states.

title: Cognitive Defusion Techniques in ACT
content: Cognitive defusion in Acceptance and Commitment Therapy involves changing one's relationship with thoughts rather than their content. Defusion techniques aim to observe thoughts as thoughts — transient mental events — rather than literal truths requiring action. Common defusion exercises include: thanking the mind ('Thank you, mind, for that thought'); labelling thoughts ('I am having the thought that I am going to fail'); repeating a word aloud until it loses meaning; the passengers on the bus metaphor (difficult thoughts are passengers, but the client remains the driver); and leaves on a stream (watching thoughts float by without engagement). Defusion reduces the emotional impact of difficult thoughts without suppression and is particularly effective for fused, repetitive negative cognitions.
short_summary: Describes ACT cognitive defusion techniques (labelling, thank-the-mind, leaves on stream) for observing thoughts as mental events rather than literal truths demanding action.

title: Values Clarification in ACT: The Vital Engagement Exercise
content: Values clarification is a core ACT process that addresses disconnection from meaningful engagement occurring when avoidance and psychological inflexibility dominate. Values are distinguished from goals: goals are specific achievable endpoints, while values are ongoing directional qualities of action (e.g., 'being a caring parent' rather than 'attending my child's concert'). Vital engagement exercises include: (1) the values bull's-eye (rating current engagement vs importance across life domains); (2) the eulogy exercise (describing qualities and contributions one would want remembered); (3) the life compass (mapping valued directions across work, relationships, health, leisure, community); and (4) identifying concrete behavioural expressions of each value in the coming week. Values clarification motivates exposure and behaviour change by connecting action to personal meaning.
short_summary: Explains ACT values clarification tools (bull's-eye, eulogy exercise, life compass) for connecting behaviour change to personal meaning and purpose.

title: Coping Cards in CBT
content: Coping cards (also called coping reminder cards or flashcards) are brief, personalised written tools developed collaboratively in session and used independently by clients in high-anxiety situations or between sessions. A standard coping card has three sections: (1) the automatic thought or feared situation; (2) the evidence-based rational response developed in therapy; (3) a personalised coping action plan ('What I will do'). Cards are kept accessible (wallet, phone photo) for use in the moment. Coping cards are particularly valuable because they provide the rational perspective during high-arousal states when self-generated restructuring is difficult. Regular review and revision keeps them relevant to current challenges.
short_summary: Describes coping cards — personalised thought-and-coping-plan summaries — as between-session CBT tools for maintaining rational perspective in high-anxiety moments.

title: The CBT Cognitive Triad in Depression
content: Beck's cognitive triad describes three domains of negative automatic thinking in depression: (1) negative view of the self ('I am worthless, defective, unlovable'); (2) negative view of the world ('The world is unfair, demanding, and defeating'); (3) negative view of the future ('Nothing will ever change; things will only get worse'). The triad operates together: negative self-view generates hopelessness; negative world view generates helplessness; negative future view sustains hopelessness and reduces motivation. CBT for depression systematically targets all three domains through thought records, behavioural experiments, and core belief work. Psychoeducation about the triad helps clients recognise these patterns as symptoms of depression rather than accurate perceptions of reality.
short_summary: Explains Beck's cognitive triad (negative views of self, world, and future) in depression and CBT strategies targeting all three domains.
source_name: Beck, Rush, Shaw & Emery CBT for Depression

title: Schema Therapy: Early Maladaptive Schemas
content: Early maladaptive schemas (EMS) are pervasive, enduring themes developed in childhood and maintained across adult life that contribute to psychological disorders. Young's schema therapy identifies 18 schemas in five domains: disconnection and rejection (abandonment, mistrust, emotional deprivation, defectiveness, social isolation); impaired autonomy (dependence, vulnerability, enmeshment, failure); impaired limits (entitlement, insufficient self-control); other-directedness (subjugation, self-sacrifice, approval-seeking); and overvigilance and inhibition (negativity, emotional inhibition, unrelenting standards, punitiveness). Schema therapy uses cognitive, behavioural, experiential (imagery rescripting, chair work), and therapeutic relationship techniques to heal EMS.
short_summary: Introduces Young's 18 early maladaptive schemas across five domains and schema therapy's cognitive, behavioural, and experiential healing techniques.
source_name: Young, Klosko & Weishaar Schema Therapy Manual
contraindications: Schema therapy requires an extended treatment framework; not suitable as a brief standalone intervention.

title: Thought Suppression and the Rebound Effect
content: Wegner's white bear experiments demonstrated that instructing people not to think about something paradoxically increases the frequency of that thought — the ironic rebound effect. In clinical contexts, attempts to suppress intrusive thoughts (obsessions, trauma memories, worry) typically backfire, increasing their frequency, intrusiveness, and associated distress. This explains why advice to 'just stop thinking about it' is counterproductive. CBT teaches clients about the rebound effect to motivate acceptance-based rather than suppression-based responses to intrusive thoughts: allowing thoughts to be present without elaboration or struggle, using defusion, detached mindfulness, or scheduled engagement. Reducing suppression effort reduces intrusive thought frequency over time.
short_summary: Explains Wegner's ironic rebound effect: thought suppression increases intrusive thought frequency, supporting acceptance-based CBT alternatives.
source_name: Wegner White Bear Experiments; Salkovskis CBT

title: Positive Data Log for Negative Core Beliefs
content: The positive data log (PDL) is a CBT technique developed by Fennell for modifying negative core beliefs about the self. Negative core beliefs operate as schemas that filter incoming information, discounting evidence contradicting the belief and highlighting evidence confirming it. The PDL directly counteracts this bias by having clients record small, daily instances of evidence consistent with a new, more accurate positive belief (e.g., 'I am a reasonably capable person'). Clients are taught to resist discounting ('That was just luck', 'Anyone could have done that') by applying consistent evaluative standards. Regular review of accumulated entries provides a growing body of evidence that gradually shifts the old belief rating and strengthens the new belief over time.
short_summary: Describes Fennell's positive data log: daily recording of evidence for a new positive core belief to counteract negative schema filtering in depression and low self-esteem.
